topic: A neighbor asks to cut down a tree on the property line
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sound of the knock on the door made my stomach clench. I froze mid-pour, the water sloshing over the counter. It was Mr. Henderson, my neighbor, his face a mask of polite expectation. I wiped my hands on a dish towel, my palms suddenly slick. "Oh, hello, Mr. Henderson," I managed, my voice coming out thin. I stared at the floor, tracing the pattern of the linoleum with my eyes. He leaned in, his shadow falling across the doorway, and started talking about the oak tree. Every word he spoke about the cutting felt like a tiny pebble dropped into a still pool, sending ripples I couldn't control. I kept shifting my weight, a nervous habit I couldn't shake.

[story 2]

I kept replaying the conversation in my head, the way his eyes had lingered on the tree, the casual way he mentioned needing it gone. My breath hitched in my throat, shallow and quick. When he finally finished, the silence stretched, thick and heavy, punctuated only by the distant hum of traffic. I gripped the edge of the kitchen table until my knuckles were white. I wanted to say something firm, something that shut the discussion down, but my mouth felt dry, glued shut. I kept glancing toward the window, half-expecting someone to be watching, someone to notice the tremor running through my hands.

[story 3]

The air in the hallway felt too tight. I paced back and forth across the small living room, my footsteps muffled by the thick rug. Every time I stopped, I felt a sudden urge to check the mailbox, to see if anything else had happened, any sign that this interaction was escalating beyond a simple request. When Mr. Henderson explained his intentions for the tree, I nodded too quickly, my throat tight with a strange dryness. I found myself fiddling with the loose thread on my sweater, a repetitive, meaningless motion that seemed to be the only way to anchor myself when everything else felt unsteady.
